lesson.menu_desc: Close the deal after the board agrees: recap the outcome, list the outstanding items, and agree the next steps with verb patterns -- "We agreed to proceed" / "We look forward to working together" -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Block 3 -- Closing the Deal
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Vinicius Vale Bassan | Aula 17 -- Closing the Deal

listenings.text: Carlos, you handled the tough questions well, and the board agreed to proceed. Now comes the part many people rush: closing the deal. The close is not just saying thank you. A strong close does three things, and I want you to do all three. First, recap the outcome in one clear line. Say what was decided, not everything that was discussed. For example: the board agreed to proceed with the acquisition, contingent on final due diligence. Second, name the outstanding items. Do not pretend everything is done. Be honest about what is still open, and who owns it. You might say: two items are still outstanding, the final valuation and the staffing plan. Third, agree the next steps and formalize them. This is where the grammar matters. Some verbs take the infinitive, with to: we agreed to proceed, we decided to formalize the terms, we plan to sign next week. Other verbs and phrases take the gerund, the ing form: we look forward to working together, we suggest signing on Friday, we recommend finalizing the budget first. Mixing these up is the most common mistake at this level, so be careful. After you agree the next steps, confirm the commitment and the owner of each one, then close warmly. A good close sounds like this: to recap, the board agreed to proceed, two items are outstanding, and we plan to sign next Friday. I look forward to working with you. Short, clear, and it moves the deal forward. Remember, the close is where a yes becomes an action.

Hi Carlos, it is Diana, calling after the closing session. The board signed off on moving ahead, so congratulations, that is a real milestone. Let me give you some feedback on how you closed, while it is fresh. First, the strong part. Your recap was excellent. You said in one line that the board agreed to proceed, contingent on due diligence, and everyone knew exactly what was decided. That is how a close should sound. Second, the outstanding items. You named them clearly, the final valuation and the staffing plan, and you said who owns each one. That honesty builds trust. Do not ever hide an open item to make the close sound cleaner. Third, the part to watch. The verb patterns. You said we look forward to work together, but it should be we look forward to working together, with the ing form, because look forward to is always followed by the gerund. And once you said we suggest to sign on Friday, when suggest also takes the gerund: we suggest signing. These are small slips, but the board notices. The infinitive verbs were perfect, by the way: you agreed to proceed and you planned to formalize the terms, both correct. Fourth, the commitment. You confirmed who owns each next step and you set the date, next Friday. That turned a yes into a real plan. One last note. Your close was warm without being long. You did not over-talk. Tighten the gerund patterns, keep the recap that clear, and you are ready to lead the signing. Strong finish, Carlos. Talk soon.
